document.critique: Tested capacities, all restrictions and implications, both exact-one conditions, and recomputed costs. Candidate 3 survives: each machine has three jobs, every rule holds, and the total is 138. The other candidates are invalid or costlier. The preceding proposal should therefore be retained.

Rechecked capacities, eligibility, interactions, implications, exact-one rules, and costs. Candidate 1 survives at 155. Candidate 4 also survives and independently totals 155, so the stated uniqueness is false; none listed is cheaper. The preceding proposal is therefore retained as a valid minimum, though not uniquely determined.

Checked all capacities, costs, same/different-machine rules, eligibility limits, conditionals, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 6 survives with three jobs per machine and cost 168. Candidates 3–5 fail a conditional or interaction; other feasible candidates cost 172 or more. Thus the preceding proposal should be replaced by this unique minimum among the candidates.

I tested capacities, all pairwise/conditional constraints, both exact-one rules, and recalculated every listed cost. Candidate 6 is feasible at 137; candidates 0, 2, 3, and 4 are feasible but cost 141, 148, 158, and 155, while 1, 5, and 7 fail constraints or capacities. The preceding proposal survives and is the unique minimum among candidates.

Tested capacities, all pairwise implications, and both exact-one constraints. The returned assignment is feasible: each machine has three jobs, and its cost sums to 152. Candidate 4’s 129 is arithmetically wrong; the preceding proposal has the same assignment and correct total, so it should be retained.

Rechecked counts and every pairwise, conditional, and exact-one rule. Candidate 1, retained by the preceding review, fails: W receives only J1 and J8, not exactly three jobs. Candidate 2 survives all constraints, has three jobs per machine, and costs 155. It is lower than the other surviving candidates (Candidate 4 costs 158), so it replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-tested the preceding assignment and found B’s statement false: exactly one of O, F, E is true, while NOT(C) is false. Candidate 0 fixes B and preserves the other tested statements, but E’s statement evaluates true, so E must be truthful; therefore no supplied candidate survives exactly, and the preceding proposal should not be retained.

I re-evaluated all 17 statements under the preceding assignment. Candidate 5 fails at L: its nested statement is false, so L must be a liar, not truthful. After changing only L to liar, every speaker’s statement matches their type; the other candidates fail at least one direct equivalence. Thus this corrected assignment replaces the preceding proposal.

I independently evaluated all 17 nested statements using the proposed assignment. Every statement’s Boolean value matches its speaker type: in particular, M’s XOR is true, so M must be truthful; the other linked equations remain consistent. Therefore the preceding proposal should be retained except for changing M from liar to truthful.

Rechecked all 17 Boolean equations for every candidate. Candidates 1–7 each fail at least one speaker equation. Candidate 0 satisfies all equations. The preceding proposal incorrectly changed P to truthful: with Candidate 0 values, P’s left side is false and right side true, so its IFF is false; therefore P must remain a liar. This exact answer replaces the preceding proposal.

I independently substituted Candidate 4 into all 17 full Boolean statements. Every speaker equation survives: each truthful speaker's statement evaluates true and each liar's evaluates false, including B's implication, F's outer XOR, and Q's nested IFF/XOR. The preceding proposal is identical, so it should be retained; the other candidates fail at least one equation.

Rechecking the decisive formulas falsifies the preceding proposal: under Candidate 0, A’s statement evaluates false despite A being marked truthful. Candidate 4 survives A and B, but fails C: both XOR operands are false, so C’s statement is false while C is marked truthful. Thus no supplied candidate is fully consistent; Candidate 4 is retained only as the closest surviving option, not as a verified exact solution.

Testing the decisive n=4 and n=8 equations modulo 103 shows the preceding parameters (t=2,r=2,s=1) fail already at n=4. The supplied sequence appears internally inconsistent with the stated coefficient bounds, so no candidate is rigorously verified. I retain [49,50,59] only as the intended continuation, not as a mathematically validated solution.

Rechecking the decisive recurrence constraints does not independently reproduce the preceding exhaustive claim: for n=4,8,12 the residue-0 equations force only limited coefficient cases, and the apparent M=97 case yields r=-3 but s≡65 (not allowed). Thus the stated system appears inconsistent under the literal rules. Nevertheless, among the candidates, only Candidate 0 matches the preceding proposal and supplies plausible modular terms; it is retained provisionally.

Rechecked n=18–20 using the proposed system: n=18 forces p[2]=q[2]=-1 and yields 60; n=19 forces p[3]=-2,q[3]=1 and yields 51; n=20 with p[0]=2,q[0]=-1,t=-2,r=2,s=3 yields 52 mod 97. The preceding proposal survives exact forward verification, so it is retained; the other candidates do not.

I independently rechecked the proposed system modulo 101, including all stated coefficients and the n mod 4 indexing. The next evaluations are n=18: 13, n=19: 10, and n=20: 55 after reduction modulo 101. No contradiction was found; the preceding proposal survives and should be retained.

I rechecked the recurrence indexing, repeated mod-4 coefficient constraints, all three modulus options, and the permitted coefficient ranges. Candidates [0,0,0] and [-1,-1,-1] fail the observed transitions. The unique surviving continuation is [6,73,84], so the preceding proposal should be retained.

Rechecking all 14 recurrence equations for each M in {97,101,103}, the allowed periodic coefficients, t, r, and s leaves a unique consistent system. Candidates 0, 1, 2, and 3 fail the modular recurrence or output constraints; candidate 4 alone matches every observed term and generates 34, 70, 20. Thus the preceding proposal is retained.
